entry.title: Re: Public Cloud 2602 / Can you define a standard Company...
entry.content:
Hello @LukasKuhn,

please see below my input:

 

1) “As far as i know, if permitted, the user is able to customize his own "My Home" Page and hide or add different functions.”

→ Correct, according to SAP’s “Make it Mine” approach users can customize their own "My Home" Page. See here for more info: My Home in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal, Section “Overview”.

 


2) “I also know about the App "Launchpad Settings" and the settings that can be made there.”

→ As an administrator, during business role management you can create Launchpad Spaces and Launchpad Pages which you can assign to business roles. It is also possible to assign the same space / page to several business roles (or all of your business roles if needed). Example below:

 

3) “My question is, is it possible to define a standard layout for "My Home" for all users in the system and prohibit them from changing it ?”

→ As an administrator you can preconfigure the "My Home" Page by using UI Adaptation as described here: My Home in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal, Section “Administrator Settings”. However, my understanding is that even after UI Adaptation you cannot prohibit the users from performing subsequent, additional changes because of the above-mentioned “Make it Mine” approach.

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: From Manual Testing to AI-Assisted QA in SAP: A Practical Journey
entry.content:
Introduction

Over the last couple of years working in SAP testing, especially around SAP Analytics Cloud, I’ve seen how much effort still goes into repetitive tasks like writing test cases, debugging failures, and preparing data.

Recently, I started experimenting with AI tools like Claude to see if they could actually help in day-to-day QA work. Not in a “replace testers” way, but more like a support system.

This blog is my honest experience of where AI helped, where it didn’t, and what I learned along the way.

How I Got Here

I started with manual testing, validating features, doing regression cycles, checking dashboards, and making sure things don’t break.

Later, I moved into automation using Selenium and mobile testing. While that reduced effort, it also introduced new challenges like debugging flaky tests, handling dynamic UI changes, and maintaining scripts.

Over time, I realized a lot of my work involved repetitive thinking. That’s when I started trying AI in small, practical ways.

Where AI Helped in My Daily Work

Writing Test Scenarios

I started by giving a JIRA story or feature description to AI and asking for test scenarios.

It usually gave a good starting point with positive scenarios, negative scenarios, and a few edge cases I might have missed.

I still review and refine everything, but it saves time when starting from scratch.

Debugging Automation Failures

Automation failures can be time-consuming to analyze, especially with long logs.

I found it useful to paste errors and ask for possible causes. It often pointed to things like timing issues, stale elements, or locator problems.

It doesn’t always give the exact answer, but it helps narrow down where to look.

Generating Test Data Ideas for SAC

In SAC testing, meaningful data is important to properly validate dashboards and charts.

AI helped me think of better datasets, account hierarchies, and different data combinations like negative values or nulls.

This made my testing more realistic instead of just validating basic cases.

A Small Real Example

One practical use case where I started experimenting with AI was analyzing gaps between customer issues and internal bugs in SAC modeling.

Normally, this would mean going through SNOW tickets and JIRA bugs manually and trying to identify patterns, which can be quite time-consuming.

I tried giving both datasets to AI and asking for a comparison. It did highlight some useful patterns like low customer issues but high internal bugs, Import and Upload as a potential high-risk area, and version-related issues showing up multiple times.

That said, it wasn’t perfect. Some insights were a bit generic, and I still had to validate and refine the output to make it meaningful.

But as a starting point, it helped me quickly get a direction and reduce the initial effort. I’m still experimenting with this approach and trying to fine-tune how I use it better

Where AI Didn’t Help Much according to my understanding

Understanding Business Context

AI doesn’t fully understand SAP-specific flows, customer use cases, or OEM integrations. Sometimes the output looks correct but doesn’t fit the actual requirement.

Generic Responses

There were cases where the answers were too high level or repetitive and missed important edge cases.

Handling Complex Issues

For deeper problems like backend issues, integration failures, or data inconsistencies, AI support is limited.

Quality Depends on Input

I noticed that the results depend a lot on how clearly the problem is described. Better input gives better output.

What I Learned

AI works best as a helper, not a replacement.

It is useful when I am stuck, when I want to speed up repetitive work, or when I need a starting point.

But it still requires validation and domain understanding.

Conclusion

From my experience, AI can definitely support QA work, but it cannot replace the role of a tester.

In SAP environments like SAC, understanding the product and business context is still the most important part.

AI just helps make some parts of the process faster and easier. I’m still exploring this space, but here’s what I’ve learned so far..  🙂  hoping to come up with a part 2!

entry.title: Re: UMB operation and CKMLCP: PUP goes back to old price
entry.content:
Hello @JavierIbanez ,

what you describe is the correct behavior. You always have to differentiate between plan and actual prices.

The material standard price is a plan price with which the current inventory and the goods movements are valuated. This price can be changed via MR21 or via the release of a cost estimate (CK24, CK40N).

The PUP is the actual material price which is only calculated and available at period end. If you have a 3S material, you have both, the standard price and the PUP for the material. You influence this price through the identified variances (procurement, production and manual material debit/credits via MR22).

In the actual costing cockpit (CKMLCP) you have two options:

  1. Utilize the actual costing process to determine the actual COGM value for the materials and make the necessary adjustment postings for the key date of period end (e.g. posting to LKW account on the last day of the closing period or posting on the normal inventory account by changing the price control from S to V for the closed period ("Revaluate material" flag)). These effects will be reversed or do not exist in the next period and the standard price remains unchanged.
  2. Mark the PUP as new standard price for the next period in the last step ("Mark Prices") of CKMLCP and release these future prices (CKME) to the standard price for the next period. This change of price will persist in the next periods and all movements will be valuated with this new price after release.

To you question "Does that make sense?":

Yes it does. You have to think about it from the following perspective: actual costing determines the actual procurement/manufacturing prices for the materials. Just because you manually change the standard price of the material this does not change the previous procurement/production values of the existing inventory. In your example, the correct price for the existing inventory remains 1000 EUR for the 1000 pieces. As stated above, if you want to influence the actual price, you have to use the material debit/credit functionality, which does not change the standard price of materials (price of V-price controlled materials will change).

I hope this helps. Please mark this as the accepted answer if you are happy. Thanks.

Best regards
Marvin

entry.title: Crystal Reports Java Runtime: Fonts not correctly loaded on Linux
entry.content:
This is a follow up to https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/how-to-avoid-nullpointerexception-with-crystal-reports-java-libraries-on/qaq-p/13728522

As of SP30 of SAP Crystal Reports, version for Eclipse the option to define the font directories via SystemFontPath was removed on favor of loading /etc/fonts/fonts.conf

This new mechanism does not work as expected because:
1. fonts.conf can have an include-tag to include additional files (e.g. from conf.d) which is ignored
2. a typical font.conf seems to contain

<dir>/usr/share/fonts</dir>
<dir>/usr/X11R6/lib/X11/fonts</dir> <dir>/usr/local/share/fonts</dir>
<dir prefix="xdg">fonts</dir>
<!-- the following element will be removed in the future -->
<dir>~/.fonts</dir>

dir prefix="xdg" is handled as directory (seems wrong because it is a relative path)

Also directories are not loaded recursive - but font files are usually in subfolders

> ls -l /usr/share/fonts/ /usr/share/fonts/truetype/
/usr/share/fonts/:
insgesamt 20
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 cmap
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 cMap
drwxr-xr-x 7 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 truetype
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 type1
drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 X11

/usr/share/fonts/truetype/:
insgesamt 20
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 dejavu
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 droid
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 liberation
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Nov  1  2020 msttcorefonts
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Mär  3  2020 noto

> ls -l /usr/share/fonts/truetype/msttcorefonts/*.ttf | wc -l
60

 It would be nice if this could be fixed with the next SP.

entry.title: BTP Trial CF UI5 App — All Destination Calls Failing with ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443 Since Today
entry.content:
BTP Trial CF App — All Destination Calls Failing with ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443 Since Today

Environment: BTP Trial, CF Runtime, ap21, Node.js approuter (@sap/approuter v20.8.1)

Issue:

Since today (7 May 2026) all destination calls from my CF application are failing with the following error:

 
 
Error while retrieving destination XXXX from destination service connect ECONNREFUSED 57.155.52.252:443

This affects every destination configured in my subaccount. Nothing changed on my side — no code changes, no config changes, no redeployments prior to this breaking.

What I have checked:

  • All services are correctly bound — destination, xsuaa, html5-apps-repo-runtime all present in VCAP_SERVICES
  • Destination Check Connection from BTP Cockpit passes successfully for all destinations
  • The destination URLs are reachable from the internet and from BAS terminal
  • Full MTA redeploy done multiple times — same result
  • Destinations deleted and recreated — same result
  • CF SSH is disabled on trial so cannot test egress directly from the container
  • BTP status page shows no incidents

Root cause hypothesis:

The destination service is resolving destination hostnames to 57.155.52.252 when called from inside CF trial containers, and that IP is unreachable from CF trial container egress. The destination service Check Connection passes because it runs from a different backend — but the actual approuter running in the CF container gets ECONNREFUSED when trying to reach that IP outbound on 443.

Question:

Has anyone else seen this today on ap21 trial? Is there a known network security group or routing change on BTP trial CF that would cause all destination calls to fail with ECONNREFUSED on this specific IP? Any workaround available?

What I tried?

# Cut the connection
cf unbind-service appName dest-svc

# Re-establish the link
cf bind-service appName dest-svc

# Force internal traffic to stay internal
cf set-env appName no_proxy .ap21.hana.ondemand.com,.hana.ondemand.com,localhost

# The manual bypass if the service itself is acting up
cf set-env appName destinations '[{"name":"DEST_NAME", "url":"YOUR_CPI_URL", "forwardAuthToken": true}]'

cf restage appName

# Spin up a second instance on a new physical server
cf scale appName -i 2

# kill the old broken instance
cf scale appName -i 1

Deleted the app in space, rebuilt, updated the ui5/cli , approuter nodemodules to latest , re deployed.

App opens but the IP thats shown in error is the destination service IP 

URL - https://destination-configuration.cfapps.ap21.hana.ondemand.com/ this resolves to the IP in the error text

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in pract
entry.content:
@vinaymittal 

Very good, the same applied for API management - KVM + Javascript Policy the GDCR + DDCR, this is not overengineering, what I proposal in the other blogs and in both cases for APIM - Proxy and CPI the iflow became immutable, no versioning, no redeploy nothing. What grows ? KVM for APIM and VM para CPI.

About one blog there is similiatries, but what I'm proposing here is more than just simple routing, is architecture definition between the first routing, acts as gateway to the Domain Layer, where the SDIA and ODCP - Reference implementation for SAP CPI lives.

In case that sample you provide - Message Types: ORDERS05 is used with various message types, including ORDERS (Order), ORDCHG (Order Change), and ORDRSP (Order Response)

Using SDIA and ODCP all those idocs goes to one package - acme.sales.o2c.integration, than inside the package, there is the local routing for only sales.o2c, iflows that belongs to sales.o2c.

The key itself should be define by the company based on the needs, can be even more fields but I belive 4 combinations of the header is enought.

Cya.

entry.title: Re: Data archive wizard no disk space
entry.content:
My guess is that the data archive wizard is using temp folder (of c:) to make pre validation prior to the operation. So the message is misleading. You could try:

Create a temp folder on your F: drive (or E: drive with 180 GB free)

Right-click This PC → Properties → Advanced System Settings

Click Environment Variables

Change both TEMP and TMP variables to point to your new folder (e.g., F:\temp)

Restart SAP Business One completely

Run the Data Archive Wizard again

 

Best regards,

 

William Wachholz (https://williamwachholz.vercel.app/about)

entry.title: Re: Custom ATP checking rule for deliveries VL01N
entry.content:
Hello @RaghavanKathamuthu 

That is another question that would merit a new thread. Still let me provide some suggestions.

First, you need to close the original sales process. The batch is faulty and it can't be delivered and invoiced. I guess you can delete the outbound delivery and reject the sales order with a dedicated rejection reason like "poor quality".

I'd suggest a completely separate SD document flow to ship restricted-use batches, i.e. new sales order type, new item types. That process needs to be to first agreed with the customer and then closely monitored and managed. You could skip ATP checks here and enter the restricted-use batch already in the sales order. This way you make sure that only this batch will be shipped. Before that I guess you would need to transfer the batch from blocked stock to unrestricted use stock. That can be done directly in MIGO. Of course, the batch will stay in restricted-use status, so it will prevent it from being assigned to other regular deliveries. That can be implemented with a batch determination and check strategy in regular deliveries.

Best regards

Dominik

entry.title: Deploy initial data from CSV on a field with @readonly annotation
entry.content:
Hi CAP community,

I am trying to update our CDS model by using @readonly annotation for one of our fields.

My question here is: will this impact deploying initial data for that field for that field using CSV file ? should I expect a regeneration of this table artifact with a different column definition when using @readonly annotation?

BR,

entry.title: Re: Managing Pre-approvals using Attendance Quota in SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
@rafledeganck 

It is only possible to check during run time whether an Attendance Quota is valid or not for that day. There is no check possible on the time recording profile. I would suggest having a flow to check if attendance quota is valid. If valid, execute time valuation chain and if not valid, execute another time valuation chain.

entry.title: SO10 text format - in continuation of case - 390687/2026
entry.content:
This is in continuation of case - 390687/2026 .

We checked the NOTE which was mentioned in previous case and following is our observation.

We have reviewed the email generation logic in detail and confirmed that this behavior is standard SAP logic. The email body is created using CL_DOCUMENT_BCS=>CREATE_DOCUMENT with content type TXT, which inherently does not support formatted text such as bold or underline. This is standard SAP design and aligns with SAPconnect behavior as described in SAP Note 171698.

entry.title: Re: SAP Crystal Reports libcurl.dll Vulnerabilities
entry.content: Hi Don, I have to disagree with "we are not using it, so it's not vulnerable". The problem is that you are delivering a vulnerable dll. Other potentially malicious software might be using your vulnerable dll to increase it's attack vector. I understand that Crystal itself is not using certain functionalities but the vulnerable libcurl that you deliver might be making it easier for an attacker.

entry.title: Re: Due Date from MRP recommendation in SAP Business One...

Hi,

Please check how your calendar is set up. Most likely you have weekend set up as Friday-Sunday, which means MRP is not taking Friday into account when calculating the due date.

Matija

entry.title: Re: Too New to Rate in Variable Pay not showing -1971
entry.content: The ratings are stored with the value of -1971 for too new to rate and -999999 for unrated. This is expected behavior. The discrepancy may be due to how the field, rating string is configured, which is not shown. if you want a workaround, you can use conditional logic to make the desired value appear. The key question is, what are you trying to accomplish? What is the requirement you are trying to satisfy?

entry.title: Re: Too New to Rate in Variable Pay not showing -1971
entry.content:
Hello @DavidSomelofske both rating to string/string rating fields are configured the same way. I will insert here prints so you can see.

There are two objectives that are dependent on the -1971 showing.

The first is that in the guidelines we need to use the rating to assigned specific guidelines to users (for example, Too New to Rate might have different guidelines than unrated and N/A). If all are 0 then in the guidelines we can't distinguish them.

Another objective that we must build a field that is shown to managers in which they will see the correct label of the grade assigned to them because in the field that comes from performance it shows label for one the forms but number for others. With this new field we would standardize all labels. For that we need to distinguish between too new to rate and unrated and N/A which is impossible if all are 0.

 

This is the field that should be shown to managers and not the standard rating field. 

We wish to understand why in Compensation template the -1971 is showing but no in Variable Pay template....

entry.title: Re: Automate log level commands - SAP CPI

entry.title: Re: What is MSEG-BUSTW
entry.content:
Functionally speaking, I don't know.

Technically speaking, the values of MSEG-BUSTW correspond to the subroutine names of the program SAPLMBGB (function group MBGB), e.g. the value WE06 corresponds to the subroutine WE06 of SAPLMBGB. These subroutines are called by (at least) the subroutine WERTE_RECHNEN of program SAPLMBGB: "PERFORM (mseg-bustw) IN PROGRAM saplmbgb."

One list of possible values are defined by these constants defined in the include MM07MCWS:

 

CONSTANTS:bustw_bnkr LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'BNKR', "Str BNK Rückstell
          bustw_bnkg LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'BNKG', "Str BNK Gegenbuch
          bustw_mb07 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'MB07', "Str Nachbelast. LB
          bustw_mb08 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'MB08', "Str Nachbe Wirkstf
          bustw_wa04 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WA04', "String Umbuchung
          bustw_wa01 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WA01', "String Warenausg
          bustw_wa06 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WA06', "Str Umbuch. Konsi
          bustw_wa10 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WA10', "Str Eigen aus Konsi
* begin note 409343
*         BUSTW_WE06 LIKE T156SY-BUSTW VALUE 'WE06'. "Str WE BE kontiert
          bustw_we06 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WE06', "Str WE BE kontiert
          bustw_we01 LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'WE01', "Str WE Bestellung
          bustw_txbr LIKE t156sy-bustw VALUE 'TXBR'. "Str tax WE Brasil

 

According to the comments (automatically translated from German by Google translate):

  • BNKG
    • Not a subroutine. Maybe not a possible value of MSEG-BUSTW.
  • BNKR
    • There's no subroutine so maybe it's not a possible value of MSEG-BUSTW.
  • MB07
    • Subsequent charge for material
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Consumption
      • Price difference For reverse postings, price control 'S' and changed standard price.
      • For posting amount specified and price control 'S'.
    • Additionally, the inventory value for the GLD price update is set in the BUALT field. (Only for S-price-controlled material).
  • MB08
    • Subsequent charge for material
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Consumption
      • Price difference
        • For reverse postings, price control 'S' and changed standard price.
        • For posting amount specified and price control 'S'.
    • Additionally, the inventory value for the GLD price update is set in the BUALT field. (Only for S-price-controlled material).
  • TXBR
    • Storage during relocation between plants (Brazil)
    • Possible postings:
      • ​​Taxes
        • Inflow/Outflow Plant 1, Plant 2
  • WA01
    • Goods withdrawal for different account assignments
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Consumption
      • Price difference
        • For reverse postings, price control 'S' and changed standard price.
        • For posting amount and price control 'S'.
    • Additionally, the inventory value for the GLD price update is set in the BUALT field. (Only for S-price-controlled material).
  • WA04
    • Plant-to-plant stock transfer
    • Possible journal entries:
      • Inventory
        • receipt/issue Plant 1
      • Inventory
        • receipt/issue Plant 2
      • Stock transfer expense
        • For price control 'S' and different plant prices.
  • WA06
    • Withdrawal of goods from consignment stock to own stock
    • Possible journal entries:
      • Consignment liability
        • Quantity
        • Consignment price
      • Inventory
        • Quantity - Price if applicable ('S')
        • Quantity - Consignment price or external stock if applicable ('V')
      • Consignment expense
        • Difference between consignment liability and inventory entry
  • WA10
    • Withdrawal of goods from consignment stock to own stock
    • Possible journal entries:
      • Inventory
        • Quantity * Consignment Price (if applicable)
        • Quantity * Consignment Price or External Order (if applicable)
      • Consignment Liability
        • Quantity * Consignment Price
      • Consignment Expense
        • Difference between consignment liability and inventory entry
  • WE01
    • Goods receipt postings for stock material
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • GR/IR reconciliation
      • Price difference:
        • If, with standard price control, the receipt value differs from the inventory posting
      • Freight share
    • With standard price control, the inventory value for the GLD price update is also set in the MSEG-BUALT field.
  • WE06
    • Goods receipt postings for stock material that is immediately converted to consumption via account assignment from the purchase order.
    • Possible postings:
      • ​​Consumption cost account from the purchase order item
      • GR/IR settlement

Other possible values according to the existing subroutines:

  • COBR
    • Consignment (Brazil)
    • Possible postings:
      • ​​FI documents
      • Taxes
  • WA03
    • Withdrawal of goods from consignment stock for consumption
    • Possible journal entries:
      • Consignment liability
        • Quantity * Consignment price
      • Consumption
        • Quantity * Sales price or external amount
      • Consignment expense
        • Difference between consignment liability and consumption entry
  • WA11
    • Goods withdrawal for a subcontractor order item
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Consumption
      • Price difference
        • For reverse postings, price control 'S', and a changed standard price.
        • For posting amount and price control 'S'.
    • Additionally, the inventory value for the GLD price update is set in the BUALT field. (Only for S-price-controlled material).
  • WA14
    • Free delivery
    • Possible booking options:
      • ​​Inventory
      • Price difference
  • WA15
    • Goods withdrawal for different account assignments on fixed assets
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Fixed asset
      • Price difference
        • For reverse postings, price control 'S', and a changed standard price.
        • For posting amount and price control 'S'.
    • Additionally, the inventory value for the GLD price update is set in the BUALT field. (Only for S-price-controlled material).
  • WA16
    • Withdrawal of goods from consignment stock for fixed asset
    • Possible journal entries:
      • Consignment Liability
      • Fixed Asset
      • Consignment Expense
  • WA21
    • Margin-neutral revaluation of product groups
    • Possible accounting entries:
      • Inventory
      • Revaluation
  • WA22
    • Profit-effective revaluation of product groups
    • Possible accounting entries:
      • Inventory
      • Revaluation
  • WF01
    • Goods receipt postings for warehouse material for the PPS order.
    • Possible postings:
      • Inventory
      • Production output
      • Price difference
        • For reverse postings when the standard price has changed.

Each subroutine "MSEG-BUSTW" calculates values according to a specific logic and stores them in global variables. Then each subroutine calls the subroutine AUSGABE of SAPLMBGB, which processes the lines of the table T156W where BUSTW = MSEG-BUSTW, reads the values of the global variables whose name is the one defined in the column T156W-FELDN, and transfers these values to the internal table XZCXCM. These values will be later transferred to MSEG. Example of some SAPLMBGB global variables (whose names are also in T156W-FELDN): VERBR, VWERE...

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in pract
entry.content:
@vinaymittal 

Receiver Layer - First Routing

Domain Layer - Second Routing + Second VM for the Domain that belongs and the Iflows that belongs only to that Package.

This principal SDIA + GDCR + ODCP reduce the ploriferation of proxies, packages and other artefacts in SAP BTP

 

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 LAYER 1 — RECEIVER LAYER (Immutable · Zero Hardcode · Static iFlow)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

 Package : acme.core.shared.integrations
 iFlow   : id01.shared.sap.idoc.pd.f.in.async
 VM      : vm.shared.idoc.routing

 ┌────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
 │    SAP     │      │   IDoc Metadata    │      │          processData()           │
 │   Sender   │─────▶│   Extract XPath    │─────▶│      composite key assembly      │
 │            │      │                    │      │                                  │
 │   HTTPS    │      │  IDOCTYP           │      │   IDOCTYP  · MESTYP              │
 │  IDoc SOAP │      │  MESTYP            │      │   SNDPOR                         │
 └────────────┘      │  SNDPOR            │      │   key: ORDERS05.ORDCHG.SAPPN1    │
                     └────────────────────┘      └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                                                  │
                                                                  ▼
                                                         ◇────────────────◇
                                                         │     Router     │
                                                         ◇────────────────◇
                                                          │              │
                                   is_mapped = true ──────┘              └──── is_mapped != true
                                         │                                           │
                                         ▼                                           ▼
                                 ┌─────────────┐                    ┌───────────────────────────┐
                                 │     End     │                    │    buildErrorPayload()    │
                                 └──────┬──────┘                    │                           │
                                        │ (ProcessDirect)           │  composite_key            │
                                        ▼                           │  vm_artifact              │
                           ┌───────────────────────┐                │  action_required          │
                           │     ProcessDirect     │                └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                           │   ${target_channel}   │                              ▼
                           └───────────┬───────────┘                ┌─────────────────┐
                                       │                            │  ✗  Error End   │
                                       │  ⚠ FAIL — check VM:        │     HTTP 500    │
                                       │  · key : ORDERS05.ORDCHG   │  IDoc status 02 │
                                       │          .SAPPN1           │  WE02 / WE05    │
                                       │  · add address in VM       └─────────────────┘
                                       ▼
             ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
             │                          acme                            │
             │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
             │  │ logistics · le-wm  → /i00/pd/acme/logistics/le-wm  │  │
             │  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
             │  │ logistics · le-shp → /i00/pd/acme/logistics/le-shp │  │
             │  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
             │  │ sales · o2c       → /i00/pd/acme/sales/o2c        │◀─┼── ORDERS05.ORDCHG.SAPPN1
             │  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
             │  │ manufacturing · p2p→ /i00/pd/acme/manu/p2p         │  │
             │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
             └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                       │
                                       │ ProcessDirect internal
                                       ▼

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
 LAYER 2 — DOMAIN LAYER (SDIA Governed · Independent Ownership)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

entry.title: Re: situation handling log error Exception in RSM API
entry.content: We had the same issue with error in situation handling. The reason was that when you create Responsibility Rule and then assign it to the Responsibility Context as Agent Rule system does not controll any modifications that you may do to the Responsibility Rule. For example in our case initial creation and assignment of the rule we changed it later on. And if you check Responsibility Context after your changes you would see errors there.

INETUM Team

entry.title: The Future of B2B Commerce Is Here — Are You Ready? Key Takeaways from B2B Online Chicago 2026
entry.content:
B2B Online Chicago 2026 brought together some of the sharpest minds in manufacturing and distribution— CMOs who’ve built digital ecosystems from scratch, marketers redefining customer experience, and eCommerce leaders finding new ways to compete in a rapidly changing landscape. The event wasn’t just about technology trends. It was about growing as leaders while tackling the very real, very complex challenges of digital transformation in B2B.

Here are the themes that dominated the conversation and why they matter for suppliers doing business in today’s connected commerce world.

Flexibility and Scalability Aren’t Optional Anymore

One of the clearest messages from the conference: a rigid commerce strategy is a liability. B2B purchasing is inherently complex— multiple stakeholders, layered approval processes, custom pricing, and diverse buyer needs mean that a one-size-fits-all approach simply doesn’t cut it.

Manufacturers and distributors need commerce architectures that can flex with their customers’ needs, scale with business growth, and adapt as market conditions shift. That means investing in platforms and networks that support multiple buying channels, contract structures, and purchasing workflows, whether a buyer is placing a routine order or navigating a complex procurement cycle.

For suppliers on SAP Business Network, this flexibility is built into how they do business. Whether engaging with buyers directly on the network or reaching customers through connected commerce channels off the network, SAP Business Network enables suppliers to meet buyers where they are without sacrificing operational efficiency or consistency.

Your Catalog Is Your Storefront— Enrich It Like One

Getting your product catalog in front of buyers sounds simple. Doing it well is anything but.

Conference conversations made it clear that catalog quality is a competitive differentiator. Enriched product data (accurate descriptions, complete attributes, consistent categorization, rich imagery) isn’t just about aesthetics. It’s about discoverability, conversion, and trust. Buyers can’t purchase what they can’t find, and they won’t purchase what they can’t confidently understand.

Suppliers who invest in their catalog data are investing in their ability to compete. On SAP Business Network, a well-maintained, enriched catalog means your products are visible, searchable, and actionable for the buyers you’re trying to reach.

The Age of AI Procurement Agents Is Coming and Your Data Needs to Be Ready

Perhaps the most forward-looking theme of the conference was the rise of AI agents, and in particular, AI procurement agents— autonomous systems that will increasingly do the buying on behalf of human purchasers. This isn’t a distant hypothetical. It’s a near-term reality that’s already reshaping how procurement teams operate.

Here’s what that means for suppliers: when an AI agent is doing the procuring, your data is your sales rep. There’s no relationship, no phone call, no persuasive pitch— just your online presence. If your data is incomplete, inaccurate, or inconsistent with what buyers are searching for, you’re invisible. If it’s rich, accurate, and well-structured, you win the transaction before a human ever gets involved.

This makes accurate supplier profiles and catalog data on SAP Business Network more critical than ever. Your data needs to clearly communicate what makes you different, match the language and attributes buyers (and their AI agents!) are searching for, and reflect your true capabilities. 

AI Is Here, But So Are People 

The conference didn’t shy away from the nuance of AI adoption. Yes, AI is transforming B2B commerce. But the consensus was clear: humans aren’t going anywhere.

In industries where purchasing decisions carry higher risk, such as complex equipment, specialized components, regulated products, and high-value contracts, buyers still want to work with people. Trust is built between humans, and that trust is hard to replicate algorithmically. There are categories and contexts where full automation isn’t appropriate, and pushing AI into those spaces without acknowledging the human element can erode buyer confidence.

Equally important: you can’t automate what you haven’t already structured. Trying to layer AI onto undefined processes, incomplete data, or unclear business logic doesn’t create efficiency— it creates chaos at scale. Organizations that are succeeding with AI in B2B are the ones that did the foundational work first: cleaning their data, documenting their processes, and developing deep subject matter expertise before asking AI to augment it.

This is a lesson that applies directly to how suppliers should think about their presence on SAP Business Network. Structured, accurate, well-governed data and profiles aren’t just good practice. They’re the prerequisite for leveraging AI effectively.

Confronting AI’s Real Concerns Head-On

Excitement about AI was balanced with honest conversation about its risks. The top concerns raised by B2B leaders at the conference were:

  • Data security: Who has access to your data, and how is it protected?
  • Response accuracy: Can you trust what AI tells you or acts on?
  • Decision transparency: Do you know why an AI made a particular decision?
  • Job displacement: What happens to the people currently doing these tasks?
  • Deployment cost: Is the ROI real, and can mid-market companies afford to compete?

These aren’t reasons to avoid AI, but instead are reasons to approach it thoughtfully. Organizations that acknowledge these concerns and build governance frameworks around them will be the ones that deploy AI responsibly and sustainably.

Jobs Aren’t Disappearing — They’re Evolving

On the workforce side, the message from B2B Online Chicago was ultimately optimistic. AI doesn’t eliminate jobs in B2B commerce and procurement— it transforms them. Roles become more technical, more strategic, and more valuable. The marketer who used to manually manage catalog updates is now governing the data strategy that feeds AI-driven procurement. The eCommerce manager who tracked orders in spreadsheets is now overseeing intelligent automation workflows.

The professionals who will thrive are those who lean into this evolution, building skills in data governance, AI oversight, and digital strategy, rather than waiting for the transformation to happen to them.

The Bottom Line

B2B Online Chicago 2026 made one thing abundantly clear: the manufacturers and distributors who will win in this next era of commerce are those who treat their digital infrastructure (their data, their catalogs, their supplier profiles, their commerce platforms) as strategic assets.

For suppliers, the opportunity is significant. SAP Business Network sits at the center of this shift, connecting suppliers to buyers both on and off the network, enabling the flexibility and scalability modern commerce demands, and providing the foundation for the AI-powered procurement future that’s already arriving.

The question isn’t whether to engage. It’s whether your data, your profile, and your strategy are ready when a buyer, or their AI agent, comes looking.

Interested in learning how to strengthen your supplier presence on SAP Business Network and prepare for the future of B2B procurement?  Subscribe to the SAP Business Network for Suppliers tag to stay updated on new posts and be sure to check out the newly updated homepage for suppliers on SAP Community.

entry.title: Struggeling with Basis in CCM
entry.content:
Hello, 

we are using CCM for Commission and Rebate Handling.

In our pricing Procedure we have our own Basis which is reduced with the Rebate amount in case there is a Rebate Contract.

So it's looking more or less like that:

PR00                                      700 EUR

Net value                               700 EUR

Rebate Accruals                   100 EUR

BASIS for commission        600 EUR

This is working, when the Rebate Contract has been created before we create the billing document. We had the case several times now that the Rebate Contract has been created after the billing document. The effect is that the Basis for the Commission is then to high. In our example it would 700.  

The basis for commission should be deducted afterwards as the old VBOF run did it.

Maybe somebody has already head the issue and solved it? 

Thanks in advance

kind regards

Martina

entry.title: Re: Cloud Transport Management for SAP BTP ABAP Environment – The Five-System Landscape
entry.content:
Hi Huijie,


With ImportUpTo, cTMS currently does not take into account branches from previous transports of the same software-component (SWC). When a transport for Software Component A (SWC A) is imported, referencing a specific branch and commit, the target tenant checks out exactly that commit. Any previous transports of the same SWC A are skipped. This is expected behavior, as a software component cannot be checked out to multiple commits from different branches at the same time.

One remark: 
We plan to introduce an enhancement that allows ImportUpTo to be executed for a selected software component only.
With this improvement, it will be possible to perform an ImportUpTo for transports related to SWC A, while previous transports of a different software component (for example, SWC B) can be imported independently at a later point in time. This enables a more flexible and selective import strategy, especially in landscapes where multiple software components are transported in parallel.

Best regards,

Sven

entry.title: Re: Characteristic Description Blank
entry.content:
Hi Vijay. Late last week I found a note https://me.sap.com/notes/3599670/E which we implemented yesterday in EQ1 and it resolved the problem. It was all about split records.

My basis colleague implemented the note which was really quick and immediately and without having to do any other activity the description was displaying

I got distracted last week because I was advised that it could have been something to do with UDT (User Defined Text) but that was not the case

Thanks very much for Vijay for your feedback

Best regards

Roberto

 

This is what the Note says ..


Symptom
Component: Product Safety
Module: Specification Management
You use transaction CGo2 or CGo2BD to call up Specification Management. You navigate to a specification and to a property.
You notice that the system does not display the descriptor for one or more classification system characteristics.

Reason and Prerequisites

Reason
Program error The classification system characteristic, for which the name is not displayed, has record splits.

Prerequisites
For information about the validity of the corrections, see the correction instructions.

Solution
The section "Support Packages" specifies the Support Packages that contain the corrections.
Alternatively, you can implement the attached correction instructions.
Note: Record splits are not supported by EHS for classification system characteristics. The functions of the classification
system supported by EHS are described in SAP Note 728636.

entry.title: SAP BusinessObjects 1000 Oracle IN limit in Prompts - How to do splitting in Universe?
entry.content:
Dear everyone,

We have a problem with SAP BusinessObjects 4.3 SP5. The users need to be able to search with up to 10k values in a single prompt field, but due to Oracle limitation, when trying to run a query with more than 1k values in a field, it yield an Oracle 1000 IN limit error. The UI input field itself seems to take a lot of values, we tried with 100k and after 30 sec of loading it showed them in the UI.

How to overcome this limit? Ideal would be, if there was a way to split the prompt before SAP BO send it to the database, maybe in the Universe/IDP tool, like a custom variable or function or script or whatever.

Something like this:

@prompt[0:999] OR

@prompt[1000:1999] OR

@prompt[2000:1999] OR

We know that we could add 10 input fields and the users could make the chunking themselves, but it's rather tedious if you need to do that multiple times a day. Also for our company it's not an option to let the users add these 10k values to the database first.

Any idea or other workaround appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Test Guidelines when extending solution using CLT
entry.content:
Good day @Vidhi_RamanChandra 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, there is guidance on how to test extensions that is built with Custom Logic. However, I would separate this into two points:

  1. Custom Logic Tracing is not the extension itself. It is mainly a troubleshooting and validation tool used to trace whether a BAdI implementation or custom logic is called, in which sequence it is called, and what parameter values are passed.
  2. Testing should still be done as business process testing, not only technical tracing. For end to end validation, the Test Automation Tool and the Test Your Process app to create, run, and evaluate automated business process tests.

So, I recommend you:

  • Use Custom Logic to implement the BAdI or business object logic.
  • Use Custom Logic Tracing to technically confirm that the logic is triggered and the right parameters are passed.
  • Use manual business process testing or Test Your Process to confirm that the final business outcome is correct.
  • Make sure you retest the extension after upgrades, transports, or changes to the BAdI interface.

What this means in simple terms, is:

  • Custom Logic Tracing tells you whether the custom logic was called and with which values.
  • It does not replace functional, regression, integration, or UAT testing.
  • The real test should be based on the full business process where the extension is used.
  • If the extension affects posting, pricing, output, workflow, approval, or master data, the complete process should be tested from input to final document.

Here are references for more guiding details.

2699678 - How To Troubleshoot Custom Business Objects Logic - SAP for Me

3354518 - Custom Logic Tracing app: "Either this method does not have any parameters or an error occurred in the method call" - SAP for Me

3618748 - BAdI APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS does not have any importing parameters - SAP for Me

3648982 - Error message "Implementation must be republished" after upgrade in SAP S/4HANA Cloud BAdi - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Why data fragmentation limits AI in SAP landscapes
entry.content:
Good point, Nicolas. And it is not limited to SAP landscapes, it is a general challenge with business data. If your data is a mess, AI will scale that mess. You describe the consequences, that is business is losing trust.

The "garbage in, garbage out" aspect only touches the surface. It is the data architecture and semantics that must be solved. "Single source of truth" was the tag line a while ago. A number of SAP customers understood this strategic opportunity and successfully managed transformation during their move to S/4. Blogs and online articles indicate that attention is getting back on that tag line again.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026: Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions
entry.content:
Hello everyone, I am Noor Zehra Naqvi (many people call me Noor),  a Senior Director of Product Management at SAP Signavio. My focus area is where AI, data, and analytics meet.

Today I would like to introduce my SAP Sapphire session:

Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions | BTM1573

In the session I’ll share our strategy about AI Agent Excellence: how to discover, govern, maintain, and continuously improve agents so they deliver real value in business processes.

AI agents are becoming a new reality. Companies are increasing their investments, and we’re seeing more and more agents show up as part of the workforce, not only as a concept, but in real operational workflows.

From there, the key question becomes: where should you apply agents, and how do you scale them responsibly? This is where SAP Signavio helps by giving agents the right process and business context, and by supporting the guardrails organizations need so agents remain compliant, predictable, and aligned with how the business actually runs.

We’ll also dive into governance and transparency, how you can understand the impact of agents on your end-to-end processes, and how to validate conformance. For example, through what we call agent mining, you can see where agents touch a process, what variants they introduce, and whether those variants are conforming or not. This level of visibility is essential when you move from a few pilots to broad adoption.

To keep it practical, one of our customers, Albatha Group will join us to share how SAP Signavio supported their AI and AI Agents journey and helped create value in their organization. We’ll close with a sneak peek at our latest releases on Agentic AI.

Interested in learning more?

 

My biggest recommendation is: get started and experiment. Try out AI agents, see what’s possible, and quite literally get your hands dirty. At the same time, scaling agents inside an organization requires a framework: strong security, clear control mechanisms, and governance that prevents unexpected or unwanted behavior.

That’s why it’s worth investing time in understanding how SAP Signavio supports this journey, so you’re set up not just to build agents, but to operate them with confidence.

Also, beyond my own session), I’d encourage you to explore a few other Signavio-related sessions at SAP Sapphire, especially those that help you connect strategy with execution:

  • Unlocking the new frontier of agentic transformation with SAP Signavio | BTM2176
  • Deliver measurable transformation value confidently with SAP Signavio | BTM1574
  • Build a transformation capability that delivers measurable value | BTM1286
  • Crafting the future: The SAP Signavio solutions 2026 road map | BTM1715

 

Now let’s hear from you!

 

In the comments’ section, it would be great to hear where you are on your agentic AI journey:

  • How far along are you, are you still piloting, or already scaling?
  • Roughly how many agents do you have running today?
  • What are the biggest struggles, blockers and risks you’re seeing as you scale?

 

If you’re attending SAP Sapphire, I hope you’ll join the conversation, both during the event and in the comments. The shift to agentic AI is exciting, but the organizations that win will be the ones that pair innovation with strong process context, governance, and transparency.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA configuration for SAC pull of plan data
entry.content:
Hello Sivaramakrishnan,

Thank you for your feedback.

Based on your explanation my understanding is the following (and please correct if I am wrong):
(A) The revenue planning of the different lines of business are split into different SAC models.
(B) Contrary all lines of business do their cost planning in one central OPEX model.
(C) Revenue planning (from the lines of business/SAC model) and cost planning (from the one central model) are finally consolidated into another central model.

Within revenue planning (A), I foresee no problems as long as the different Lines of Business (which plan on the same revenue accounts) are distinguished via a specific S/4 field (representing the line of business), and of course, this S/4 field must be in addition included into the plan data scope.

Similarly, revenue planning (from the 10 different SAC models) and OPEX planning can be written into the same planning category as different GL accounts are used for revenue and cost planning (and GL account being part of the plan data scope).  However, ensure that any previously planned GL accounts (which were subsequently deleted in SAC) are correctly reflected in S/4 (i.e. with GL account being part of the Plan Data Scope).

In contrast, data within the consolidated financial model may overlap if plan data is (most probably) written to the same GL accounts used for revenue and OPEX planning. Moreover, it would not make sense to map the data from the Consolidated Financial Model (C) to the same category used for Revenue (A) and Cost Planning (B), as S/4 reporting could potentially display duplicate values. Therefore, it must always be clarified whether, from a reporting perspective, it is necessary/correct to write the data into a single planning category.

If the assumptions I made above are incorrect, then I want to point out that cross-combinations of the (plan data scope) field values are taken as well into account to replace the existing S/4 data (as described in section 2 under Technical Settings) with the consequence that a configuration ID (related to one model) can overwrite the data of another configuration ID (associated to another model).

Thank you.

Kind regards,
Tarkan Citirgi

P.S. The provided "import" templates (as part of our app Import Financial Plan Data) work primarily with a single planning category (the SAP-delivered planning category PLN); the budget-specific categories used trigger additional budget-specific functionalities which explains why they are utilized in the budget specific import templates.

entry.title: Re: What is the difference between a review booklet and a...
entry.content:
Good day @db11 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: What are the differences between reports and booklets, and how does SAP intend them to be used?

Answer: A report is usually best for focused analysis of one query; KPI, or dataset. A Review Booklet on the other hand is better for a guided business review across multiple pages, for example cost center review, financial statement review, group reporting review, budget review, profitability review, or period-end analysis.


Question 2: How many of the current query reports/which areas of applications are planned to be replaced with review booklets? How long will the transition take?

Answer: I am not aware of any SAP statement that confirms that all query reports will be replaced by Review Booklets, and there is no fixed transition timeline for a full replacement. SAP generally approaches this incrementally. In other words, Review Booklets are on release-by-release base, while query reports and analytical queries remain available and relevant.

I recommend you:

  • List the query reports your business currently uses.
  • Check whether a Review Booklet already exists for the same business area in Manage KPIs and Reports.  
     
  • Use dimension, filters, hierarchies, drilldowns to compare the Review Booklet with the current report. Exporting requirements is also an option.
  • You can use the Review Booklet where your business needs a guided review flow.
  • Keep your existing query report where the Review Booklet does not yet cover the required reporting your business needs. 
  • You can use Review Booklet Designer in Manage KPIs and Report to copy, or create a Review Booklet.

Here are references for more guidance

3730170 - FAQ for Cost Center Review Booklet in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3512310 - Not assigned cost center hierarchy in APP 'Cost Center review booklet' - SAP for Me

3595605 - Lease and Real Estate Accounting Review Booklet App not displaying data - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: International Design Thinking Training & Certification Bootcamp
entry.content:
For the first time, Impact Week Armenia 2026 is bringing a global Design Thinking training and certification program to Yerevan — creating a rare opportunity for Armenia’s business, education, and innovation community.

Programs of this level, usually delivered by such experienced innovation consultants and experts from SAP,  UnternehmerTUM, and former HPI program leadership, are typically priced much higher internationally. This year, because the bootcamp is taking place in Armenia, the program has been made highly affordable for local participants.

This is a non-profit initiative: all participation fees will directly support scholarships for 80+ young Armenian AI talents, helping build the next generation of innovators, entrepreneurs, and leaders of Armenia's AI ecosystem.

More details are available here:
https://gamma.app/docs/IMPACT-Week-Armenia-2026-6zz1zqzzvvwdqtm?mode=doc

Participants can choose between two tracks:

  • Design Thinking Coach Certification
    June 22–27 + 12 hours of online preparation | Application deadline: May 10
    A certification track for professionals who want to facilitate Design Thinking workshops, guide teams, and bring structured innovation methods into their organizations.
  •  4-Day Design Thinking Bootcamp
    June 24–27 | Application deadline: June 10
    A hands-on innovation training where participants work in teams on real-life challenges and learn how to move from problem understanding to solution concepts and prototypes.

This can be especially valuable for companies that want to strengthen internal innovation capacity, train future facilitators, support product and service development, or expose their teams to internationally tested innovation methods.

We are open to partnership and sponsorship opportunities with companies that would like to support this initiative and contribute to strengthening Armenia’s entrepreneurship and innovation capacity.

In case of any questions, please feel free to contact Carolin Taenzer -https://www.linkedin.com/in/carolin-taenzer/ or  Tsovinar Chugaszyan -  https://www.linkedin.com/in/tsovinarc/?skipRedirect=true
